title: What is Data Analytics
subtitle: Build the mental model before touching any tool.

topics.name: What is data?
topics.desc: Raw facts and figures with no meaning on their own — the rawest form of information before context is applied.

topics.name: What is analytics?
topics.desc: The process of turning raw data into decisions. Not just looking at numbers — asking what they mean and what to do next.

topics.name: Data vs information vs insights
topics.desc: Data is raw. Information is data with context. Insights are information that drives action. Know the difference — most analysts stop at information.

topics.name: The analytics lifecycle
topics.desc: The most critical concept in this entire roadmap. Define the question → collect data → clean → analyze → visualize → communicate → act. Every project follows this arc.

topics.name: Descriptive Analytics
topics.desc: What happened? Looking backward at historical data to understand past performance. The foundation of all analytics work.

topics.name: Diagnostic Analytics
topics.desc: Why did it happen? Drilling into root causes. Moving from reporting what occurred to understanding why.

topics.name: Predictive Analytics
topics.desc: What will happen? Using historical patterns to forecast future outcomes. Requires statistical thinking and clean data.

topics.name: Prescriptive Analytics
topics.desc: What should we do? The highest level — recommending specific actions based on predicted outcomes. Where analysts become decision partners.

topics.name: The analyst's role in a business
topics.desc: You are a translator between data and decisions. Your job is not to produce reports — it is to make the business smarter.

topics.name: How data drives decisions
topics.desc: Data does not speak for itself. You have to frame it, contextualize it, and connect it to what the business cares about.

topics.name: The modern data stack
topics.desc: Ingestion → Storage → Transformation → BI. Knowing where your tools fit in this chain makes you a far more effective analyst.

topics.name: Revenue
topics.desc: Total income generated before any costs are deducted. The top line. Always know what drives it.

topics.name: Profit & Cost
topics.desc: Revenue minus expenses. Gross profit is before operating costs. Net profit is after everything. Analysts who understand cost structure are rare.

topics.name: Gross Margin & Net Margin
topics.desc: Percentages that show how efficiently a business turns revenue into profit. Critical for comparing companies across different sizes.

topics.name: Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC)
topics.desc: How much does it cost to acquire one customer? If CAC exceeds LTV, the business is dying — even if revenue looks healthy.

topics.name: Customer Lifetime Value (LTV)
topics.desc: The total revenue a customer is expected to generate over their entire relationship with the business. Pair with CAC for the full picture.

topics.name: Churn Rate & Retention Rate
topics.desc: Churn is the percentage of customers lost in a period. Retention is the inverse. High churn destroys LTV regardless of acquisition volume.

topics.name: Conversion Rate
topics.desc: The percentage of people who take a desired action. Applies to sales funnels, marketing campaigns, and product flows.

topics.name: Operational KPIs
topics.desc: Metrics that measure how efficiently the business runs day-to-day — SLA adherence, ticket resolution time, delivery rates.

topics.name: Financial KPIs
topics.desc: Metrics that measure financial health — EBITDA, burn rate, runway, working capital. Know these before walking into any stakeholder meeting.

topics.name: Business questions vs analytical questions
topics.desc: "Why are sales dropping?" is a business question. "What is the month-over-month revenue change by region?" is the analytical version. Learn to translate between the two.

topics.name: KPI design
topics.desc: Not every metric is a KPI. A KPI is a metric tied directly to a business objective. Most dashboards have too many metrics and too few KPIs.

topics.name: Root cause thinking
topics.desc: Symptoms point to problems. Problems have causes. Do not stop at the first answer — ask "why" at least three times before presenting a finding.

topics.name: Hypothesis-driven analysis
topics.desc: Start with a hypothesis, then use data to test it. This is faster and more rigorous than exploring data without direction.

topics.name: Defining success metrics
topics.desc: Before any project starts, define what good looks like. If you cannot measure success, you cannot claim it.

topics.name: Understanding business objectives
topics.desc: Every analytical request exists to serve a business goal. If you do not know the goal, you will answer the wrong question beautifully.

topics.name: Missing values
topics.desc: Data that is absent where it should exist. Missing is not the same as zero. How you handle it changes your results entirely.

topics.name: Duplicate records
topics.desc: The same data recorded more than once. Deduplication is one of the first things you do on any real dataset.

topics.name: Invalid records
topics.desc: Data that exists but is logically wrong — a birthdate in the future, a negative price, an email with no "@". Always validate before analysing.

topics.name: Inconsistent formatting
topics.desc: The same value stored in multiple formats — "Nigeria", "NG", "NGR". Standardisation before analysis is non-negotiable.

topics.name: Outliers
topics.desc: Values that sit far outside the normal range. Sometimes they are errors. Sometimes they are the most important finding in your dataset.

topics.name: Data validation
topics.desc: Rules that enforce data quality at the point of entry. Prevention is cheaper than cleaning.

topics.name: Data governance basics
topics.desc: Who owns the data? Who can access it? How is it maintained? Governance is what separates mature data organisations from chaotic ones.

topics.name: Critical thinking
topics.desc: Question everything — including your own analysis. The data is never wrong, but your interpretation might be.

topics.name: Problem solving
topics.desc: Break complex problems into smaller, answerable questions. Structured decomposition is what separates good analysts from great ones.

topics.name: Business communication
topics.desc: The ability to explain a technical finding in one sentence that a non-technical stakeholder can act on. This is the hardest skill to develop.

topics.name: Curiosity & asking good questions
topics.desc: The best analysts are relentlessly curious. A good question saves 10 hours of analysis in the wrong direction.

title: Math & Statistics
subtitle: The language data speaks. You need to be fluent before you can interpret anything.

topics.name: Mean
topics.desc: The average. Useful but sensitive to extreme values. Always check if outliers are pulling it away from the typical value.

topics.name: Median
topics.desc: The middle value when sorted. More robust than the mean when your data has outliers or is skewed. Use it for income and pricing data.

topics.name: Mode
topics.desc: The most frequently occurring value. Most useful for categorical data — what product is ordered most, what city appears most often.

topics.name: Range
topics.desc: The distance between the minimum and maximum values. A quick measure of spread, but easily distorted by a single outlier.

topics.name: Quartiles
topics.desc: Values that divide your data into four equal parts. Q1 (25th), Q2 (50th/median), Q3 (75th). Essential for box plots and distribution analysis.

topics.name: Percentiles
topics.desc: The value below which a given percentage of data falls. The 90th percentile means 90% of values fall below that point.

topics.name: Variance
topics.desc: The average of the squared differences from the mean. Measures how spread out your data is. Higher variance means more volatility.

topics.name: Standard Deviation
topics.desc: The square root of variance. Brings variability back to the original unit of measurement. The most common measure of spread in data analytics.

topics.name: Interquartile Range (IQR)
topics.desc: Q3 minus Q1. The middle 50% of your data. Used to detect outliers — any value more than 1.5x IQR beyond Q1 or Q3 is flagged.

topics.name: Basic probability concepts
topics.desc: The likelihood of an event occurring, expressed between 0 and 1. The foundation of all statistical inference and prediction.

topics.name: Conditional probability
topics.desc: The probability of event A given that event B has already occurred. Critical for understanding dependencies in your data.

topics.name: Independent events
topics.desc: When the occurrence of one event has no effect on the probability of another. Most real-world data involves dependent events — know the difference.

topics.name: Expected value
topics.desc: The probability-weighted average of all possible outcomes. Used to make decisions under uncertainty — standard in business case analysis.

topics.name: Normal Distribution
topics.desc: The bell curve. Most natural phenomena follow it. When your data is normally distributed, the mean equals the median and powerful statistical tools apply.

topics.name: Skewness
topics.desc: A measure of asymmetry in your distribution. Positive skew means the tail is on the right (income data). Negative skew means it is on the left.

topics.name: Kurtosis
topics.desc: How fat or thin the tails of your distribution are. High kurtosis means more extreme values — important in risk and fraud analysis.

topics.name: Z-Scores
topics.desc: How many standard deviations a value sits from the mean. A Z-score above 3 or below -3 signals an outlier. Used in anomaly detection.

topics.name: Correlation
topics.desc: A measure of how two variables move together, from -1 to +1. Zero means no linear relationship. Strong correlation demands investigation.

topics.name: Covariance
topics.desc: The direction of the relationship between two variables. Positive means they move together. Negative means they move in opposite directions.

topics.name: Correlation vs causation
topics.desc: The most abused concept in analytics. Two things can be perfectly correlated and completely unrelated. Never present correlation as proof of cause.

topics.name: Sampling
topics.desc: Analysing a subset of data to draw conclusions about the whole. The quality of your sample determines the validity of your conclusions.

topics.name: Population vs sample
topics.desc: Population is every possible data point. Sample is what you actually have. Understanding which you are working with changes how you interpret results.

topics.name: Confidence intervals
topics.desc: A range of values within which the true population parameter likely falls. A 95% CI means if you repeated the study 100 times, 95 results would contain the true value.

topics.name: Statistical significance
topics.desc: The probability that your result is not due to random chance. A p-value below 0.05 is the conventional threshold — but context always matters.

topics.name: Hypothesis testing (intro)
topics.desc: A structured method for testing whether an observed effect is real or random. Null hypothesis vs alternative hypothesis. Know the framework before running tests.

topics.name: Interpreting statistical results
topics.desc: Numbers without context mislead. Always ask: is this difference meaningful to the business, not just statistically significant?

topics.name: Identifying meaningful patterns
topics.desc: Not every pattern is worth reporting. A 0.1% change in a metric that does not affect revenue is noise. Train yourself to filter for signal.

topics.name: Avoiding misleading conclusions
topics.desc: Simpson's paradox, cherry-picking date ranges, ignoring base rates — learn the common traps analysts fall into before you fall into them yourself.

title: Excel
subtitle: The most underestimated tool in the analytics stack. Master it before you dismiss it.

topics.name: Interface and navigation
topics.desc: Ribbons, cell references, keyboard shortcuts. Speed in Excel is a professional signal — slow analysts get replaced by faster ones.

topics.name: Data entry and formatting
topics.desc: How data is stored determines how it can be analysed. Numbers stored as text, dates stored as strings — formatting errors corrupt entire analyses.

topics.name: Tables and named ranges
topics.desc: Convert your data to a Table immediately. It auto-expands, supports structured references, and makes your formulas readable.

topics.name: Data validation
topics.desc: Restrict what can be entered into a cell. Dropdowns, number ranges, custom rules. Prevention is faster than cleaning.

topics.name: IF & IFS
topics.desc: Return one value if a condition is true, another if false. IFS handles multiple conditions cleanly without nesting. The most fundamental logic function.

topics.name: SUMIFS
topics.desc: Sum values that meet multiple criteria. Not SUMIF — always SUMIFS. More flexible, handles multiple conditions, same syntax.

topics.name: COUNTIFS
topics.desc: Count cells that meet multiple criteria. Pair with SUMIFS to build frequency and volume analysis across any combination of filters.

topics.name: AVERAGEIFS
topics.desc: Average values that meet multiple criteria. Useful for computing segment-level averages without building a Pivot Table.

topics.name: TEXT functions
topics.desc: LEFT, RIGHT, MID, TRIM, PROPER, CONCATENATE, TEXTJOIN. Cleaning and reformatting text data — unavoidable in real-world datasets.

topics.name: Date functions
topics.desc: TODAY, NOW, DATEDIF, EOMONTH, WEEKDAY, NETWORKDAYS. Dates are among the most common data types — and the most frequently broken.

topics.name: Logical functions
topics.desc: AND, OR, NOT, IFERROR, IFNA. Combine conditions and handle errors gracefully. Clean error handling is what separates professional spreadsheets from amateur ones.

topics.name: XLOOKUP
topics.desc: The modern replacement for every lookup function. Searches any direction, returns multiple columns, handles not-found cases natively. Learn this first.

topics.name: INDEX MATCH
topics.desc: The combination that XLOOKUP was built to replace. Still critical to understand — it works in older Excel versions and underpins how Excel retrieval logic works.

topics.name: FILTER
topics.desc: Returns an array of rows that meet your criteria. Dynamic, spills automatically, and replaces a dozen workarounds. Essential for modern Excel workflows.

topics.name: UNIQUE
topics.desc: Returns a list of unique values from a range. Combine with SORT and FILTER for dynamic, self-updating lists that never need manual refreshing.

topics.name: DGET
topics.desc: Extracts a single value from a database that matches specified criteria. Useful when you need precision retrieval without the overhead of a full lookup setup.

topics.name: Remove duplicates
topics.desc: The first thing you do on any raw dataset. Always check before removing — duplicates are sometimes intentional.

topics.name: Text to columns
topics.desc: Split a single column into multiple columns by delimiter. Handles the "first name last name in one cell" problem and countless import issues.

topics.name: Flash Fill
topics.desc: Excel recognises patterns in your data entry and fills the rest automatically. Powerful for reformatting — learns from one or two examples.

topics.name: Conditional formatting
topics.desc: Visual cues that highlight patterns, outliers, and thresholds automatically. Not decoration — a data quality and communication tool.

topics.name: Connecting data sources
topics.desc: Pull data from CSVs, databases, web pages, and SharePoint directly into Excel without copy-pasting. Every refresh pulls fresh data automatically.

topics.name: Data cleaning in Power Query
topics.desc: Remove nulls, fix data types, trim whitespace, replace values. All recorded as steps that re-run every time you refresh. This is repeatable cleaning.

topics.name: Data transformation
topics.desc: Pivot, unpivot, group by, add custom columns. Reshaping data into the structure your analysis needs before it hits the spreadsheet.

topics.name: Append queries
topics.desc: Stack multiple tables vertically — combining January, February, and March files into one dataset automatically.

topics.name: Merge queries
topics.desc: Join two tables on a common key, like a SQL JOIN inside Excel. Left join, inner join, full outer — all available in the interface.

topics.name: Pivot Tables
topics.desc: Summarise thousands of rows into a cross-tabulation in seconds. The most important analysis tool in Excel. Every analyst must be fluent.

topics.name: Pivot Charts
topics.desc: Charts connected directly to Pivot Tables. Dynamic, filterable, and automatically updated when your data changes.

topics.name: Slicers
topics.desc: Visual filter buttons connected to Pivot Tables and charts. They make your dashboards interactive without a single line of code.

topics.name: KPI dashboards
topics.desc: A single-screen view of the metrics that matter. Design for the decision, not the data — show only what drives action.

topics.name: Interactive dashboards
topics.desc: Slicers, dropdowns, and dynamic charts that respond to user input. The goal is to let the stakeholder explore without needing to ask you.

topics.name: Executive dashboards
topics.desc: Three numbers, one message, zero clutter. Executive dashboards communicate a decision — not a summary of everything you analysed.

topics.name: Copilot for Excel
topics.desc: Microsoft's native AI assistant. Generates formulas, summarises data, and creates charts from natural language prompts directly in the ribbon.

topics.name: Claude for formula generation
topics.desc: Describe what you want to calculate in plain English. Claude writes the formula, explains it, and handles edge cases you did not think of.

topics.name: ChatGPT for data cleaning
topics.desc: Paste a sample of your messy data and ask for a cleaning strategy. Gets you from raw to structured faster than debugging manually.

topics.name: Automated analysis with AI
topics.desc: Use AI to generate interpretation of your Pivot Table results, write the narrative for your dashboard, and surface patterns you might have missed.

projects.desc: Analyse social media performance across platforms, campaigns, and content types using Excel and Power Pivot.

title: SQL
subtitle: The language every data professional must speak. There is no shortcut here.

topics.name: What is a database?
topics.desc: An organised collection of structured data stored and accessed electronically. The foundation of every data system you will ever work with.

topics.name: What is an RDBMS?
topics.desc: A Relational Database Management System — software that stores data in related tables. PostgreSQL, SQL Server, MySQL, SQLite are all RDBMS engines.

topics.name: Primary Keys & Foreign Keys
topics.desc: A primary key uniquely identifies each row. A foreign key links one table to another. These relationships are what make relational databases powerful.

topics.name: Normalization
topics.desc: The process of organising data to reduce redundancy. 1NF, 2NF, 3NF. Understanding normalisation helps you understand why data is structured the way it is.

topics.name: Database schema design
topics.desc: The blueprint of your database — which tables exist, what columns they contain, and how they relate. Reading a schema is a core analyst skill.

topics.name: DDL — Data Definition Language
topics.desc: CREATE, ALTER, DROP. Commands that define or modify the structure of database objects. Analysts read DDL; engineers write it.

topics.name: DML — Data Manipulation Language
topics.desc: INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE. Commands that modify the data inside tables. Know these to understand how your data gets populated and changed.

topics.name: DQL — Data Query Language
topics.desc: SELECT is the only command. This is where 90% of your analyst work lives. Master it completely before touching anything else.

topics.name: TCL — Transaction Control Language
topics.desc: COMMIT, ROLLBACK, SAVEPOINT. Controls how changes are saved or reversed. Critical context for data integrity and understanding production systems.

topics.name: DCL — Data Control Language
topics.desc: GRANT, REVOKE. Controls access to data. Relevant when you need permissions or are working in a governed data environment.

topics.name: SELECT
topics.desc: The entry point to every query. SELECT * is for exploration only — always specify columns in production queries.

topics.name: WHERE
topics.desc: Filters rows based on conditions. The most important clause for controlling what data your query returns.

topics.name: ORDER BY
topics.desc: Sorts your result set. ASC is default. Use DESC for rankings. Always pair with LIMIT when exploring large datasets.

topics.name: GROUP BY
topics.desc: Collapses rows into groups for aggregation. Every column in SELECT that is not an aggregate function must appear in GROUP BY.

topics.name: HAVING
topics.desc: Filters after aggregation, where WHERE filters before. HAVING is how you filter on a SUM or COUNT result.

topics.name: DISTINCT
topics.desc: Returns only unique values. Use to check cardinality, deduplicate results, or explore what categories exist in a column.

topics.name: String functions
topics.desc: UPPER, LOWER, TRIM, REPLACE, SUBSTRING, CONCAT, LEN. Cleaning and manipulating text data inside SQL — avoids the need to export and clean in Excel.

topics.name: Numeric functions
topics.desc: ROUND, CEILING, FLOOR, ABS, MOD. Formatting and transforming numbers directly in the query layer.

topics.name: Date functions
topics.desc: DATEPART, DATEDIFF, DATEADD, FORMAT, GETDATE. Dates power most business analysis — cohort analysis, trend reporting, SLA tracking.

topics.name: Conversion functions
topics.desc: CAST, CONVERT, TRY_CAST. Changing data types within a query. Essential when joining tables where the same value is stored in different types.

topics.name: Aggregate functions
topics.desc: SUM, COUNT, AVG, MIN, MAX. The building blocks of every summary query. Know the difference between COUNT(*) and COUNT(column).

topics.name: CASE Statements
topics.desc: SQL's IF-THEN-ELSE. Creates new categorical columns, buckets numeric ranges, maps status codes to readable labels. Used in almost every real query.

topics.name: INNER JOIN
topics.desc: Returns only rows with matching values in both tables. The default join. If you do not specify, this is what most engines assume.

topics.name: LEFT JOIN
topics.desc: Returns all rows from the left table and matching rows from the right. Non-matches return NULL. The most common join in analytics work.

topics.name: RIGHT JOIN
topics.desc: Returns all rows from the right table and matching rows from the left. Functionally equivalent to a LEFT JOIN with tables swapped — most analysts stick to LEFT.

topics.name: FULL OUTER JOIN
topics.desc: Returns all rows from both tables, with NULLs where there is no match. Used to identify records that exist in one source but not the other.

topics.name: SELF JOIN
topics.desc: Joining a table to itself. Used for hierarchical data, comparing rows within the same dataset, or finding pairs.

topics.name: CROSS JOIN
topics.desc: Every row from the left table paired with every row from the right. Produces a Cartesian product. Use with caution — large tables explode in size.

topics.name: Subqueries
topics.desc: A query nested inside another query. Used in SELECT, FROM, and WHERE clauses. Powerful but can become unreadable — use CTEs for complex logic.

topics.name: Correlated Subqueries
topics.desc: A subquery that references the outer query. Executes once per row — slow on large datasets but necessary for certain row-level comparisons.

topics.name: Scalar Subqueries
topics.desc: A subquery that returns exactly one value. Used like a column reference — you can SELECT it, filter on it, or use it in a CASE statement.

topics.name: CTEs
topics.desc: Common Table Expressions. Named subqueries defined at the top of your query with WITH. They make complex queries readable, debuggable, and maintainable.

topics.name: Recursive CTEs
topics.desc: A CTE that references itself. Used for hierarchical data — org charts, bill of materials, category trees. Advanced but extremely powerful.

topics.name: ROW_NUMBER()
topics.desc: Assigns a unique sequential number to each row within a partition. Used for deduplication, pagination, and ranking without ties.

topics.name: RANK() & DENSE_RANK()
topics.desc: RANK leaves gaps after ties. DENSE_RANK does not. Know which one your business logic requires before choosing.

topics.name: NTILE()
topics.desc: Divides rows into N equal buckets. The foundation of percentile-based segmentation — RFM scoring, performance tiers, quartile analysis.

topics.name: LAG() & LEAD()
topics.desc: Access the value from a previous or next row within a partition. Essential for period-over-period comparisons — MoM change, rolling deltas.

topics.name: Running Totals
topics.desc: SUM() OVER (ORDER BY date) gives a cumulative sum. Ubiquitous in financial reporting, sales tracking, and progress monitoring.

topics.name: Moving Averages
topics.desc: AVG() OVER (ROWS BETWEEN n PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW). Smooths noisy time series data to surface underlying trends.

topics.name: Query execution basics
topics.desc: SQL does not execute in the order you write it. The engine optimises. Understanding execution order explains why certain queries are slow.

topics.name: Indexes
topics.desc: Data structures that speed up retrieval on specific columns. Querying an indexed column is orders of magnitude faster than a full table scan.

topics.name: Query optimization
topics.desc: Avoid SELECT *, filter early, avoid functions on indexed columns in WHERE clauses, use EXISTS over IN for large datasets.

topics.name: Performance tuning
topics.desc: Reading execution plans, identifying table scans vs index seeks, measuring query cost. The difference between a query that runs in 2 seconds and 2 minutes.

topics.name: Views
topics.desc: Saved SELECT queries that behave like virtual tables. Use views to standardise business logic, hide complexity, and control what analysts can see.

topics.name: Stored Procedures (overview)
topics.desc: Pre-compiled SQL blocks stored in the database. Understand what they are and when engineers use them — you will encounter them in production systems.

topics.name: Functions (overview)
topics.desc: Reusable SQL logic that accepts inputs and returns a value. Table-valued functions are particularly useful for parameterised reporting.

topics.name: Claude for SQL writing
topics.desc: Describe your question in plain English. Claude writes the query, explains each clause, and suggests edge cases you should handle.

topics.name: ChatGPT for SQL debugging
topics.desc: Paste a broken query and the error message. AI diagnoses the issue, explains why it is happening, and rewrites the fix.

topics.name: Query optimization using AI
topics.desc: Share a slow query with your schema context. AI identifies the bottleneck, suggests indexes, and rewrites for better execution.

topics.name: Prompt patterns for SQL
topics.desc: How to give AI enough context to write accurate queries — schema, sample data, expected output, and dialect all matter.

projects.desc: Investigate 15,000 shipments to diagnose a logistics SLA failure with EUR 4.2M contract at risk. Six analytical deliverables.

title: Data Visualization Principles
subtitle: Before you open Power BI, understand why humans see data the way they do.

topics.name: Why visualization matters
topics.desc: The human brain processes visuals 60,000 times faster than text. A well-designed chart communicates in seconds what a table communicates in minutes.

topics.name: Human perception and cognition
topics.desc: Pre-attentive attributes — colour, size, position, shape — are processed before conscious thought. Design with these and your audience sees the insight instantly.

topics.name: Data storytelling principles
topics.desc: Every chart tells a story. Context, conflict, resolution. If your visualisation does not have a message, it is decoration.

topics.name: Bar charts
topics.desc: Comparisons across categories. Horizontal bars for long labels. Always start at zero. The most misused chart type — and the most reliable.

topics.name: Line charts
topics.desc: Change over time. Requires a continuous time axis. Do not use lines for categorical comparisons — the implied continuity misleads.

topics.name: Scatter plots
topics.desc: Relationships between two numeric variables. Shows correlation, clusters, and outliers simultaneously. Underused in business dashboards.

topics.name: Histograms
topics.desc: Distribution of a single numeric variable. Not a bar chart — the bars are continuous and represent ranges, not categories.

topics.name: Heatmaps
topics.desc: Colour-encoded matrix for showing patterns across two categorical dimensions. Powerful for time-of-day analysis, correlation matrices, and geographic data.

topics.name: Waterfall charts
topics.desc: Shows cumulative effect of sequential positive and negative values. Standard for financial variance analysis — budget vs actual, revenue bridge.

topics.name: Tree maps
topics.desc: Proportional rectangles for part-to-whole relationships. Effective for product mix, category share, and portfolio composition.

topics.name: Color theory
topics.desc: Use colour to encode meaning, not decorate. Three colours maximum. Ensure accessibility for colour-blind users. Red means bad, green means good — but only if consistent.

topics.name: Visual hierarchy
topics.desc: The most important number should be the biggest thing on the screen. Guide the viewer's eye from the headline to the detail, not the other way around.

topics.name: White space
topics.desc: Empty space is not wasted space — it is breathing room that improves comprehension. Cluttered dashboards are not thorough, they are unusable.

topics.name: Accessibility
topics.desc: Design for the 8% of men who are colour-blind. Use shape and label alongside colour. High contrast. Readable font sizes. Accessible design is better design for everyone.

topics.name: Dashboard layouts
topics.desc: Top-left is where the eye lands first. Put the headline KPI there. Use a Z or F reading pattern. Group related visuals. Separate filters from content.

topics.name: Cognitive load
topics.desc: Every element on a dashboard costs the viewer mental effort. Remove anything that does not contribute to the decision. Simplicity is respect for your audience's attention.

topics.name: Information prioritization
topics.desc: Rank your insights by decision relevance. What does the person viewing this dashboard need to decide? Show that first, everything else second.

topics.name: Executive reporting design
topics.desc: Executives want one insight per screen, not twenty metrics. Design for the three-second test — if the message is not clear in three seconds, redesign.

topics.name: Finding the narrative
topics.desc: What is the one thing this data is trying to say? Start there. Build every chart around that message.

topics.name: Building insight-driven visuals
topics.desc: An insight is not "sales went up in Q3." An insight is "sales went up 34% in Q3 driven by the West region, which offset a 12% decline in the East."

topics.name: Guiding decision-making
topics.desc: The final test of any visualisation — did it lead to a decision? If not, it was a report, not an insight.

title: Power BI
subtitle: The most complete BI tool in the market. Learn it end to end — not just the visuals.

topics.name: Connecting data sources
topics.desc: Excel, CSV, SQL Server, PostgreSQL, SharePoint, REST APIs, web pages. Power Query connects to almost everything and reloads automatically.

topics.name: Data cleaning
topics.desc: Remove nulls, fix data types, trim whitespace, replace values — all recorded as applied steps that re-run on every refresh. Repeatable, auditable cleaning.

topics.name: Transformations
topics.desc: Pivot, unpivot, group by, add conditional columns, extract date parts. Shape your data into the structure your model needs before it loads.

topics.name: Merging queries
topics.desc: Join two queries on a common key — equivalent to a SQL JOIN. All join types available. The foundation of multi-source data models.

topics.name: Appending queries
topics.desc: Stack multiple tables vertically. Combine January, February, and March files into one unified fact table automatically.

topics.name: Relationships & Cardinality
topics.desc: One-to-many, many-to-one, many-to-many. The direction of your relationship determines how filters flow across your model. Get this wrong and your DAX will lie.

topics.name: Star schema
topics.desc: One fact table surrounded by dimension tables. The optimal structure for Power BI. Faster queries, simpler DAX, cleaner model.

topics.name: Snowflake schema
topics.desc: Normalised dimensions — dimension tables with their own dimension tables. More complex than star schema. Understand it to know why star is usually better.

topics.name: Fact tables
topics.desc: Store measurable events — sales transactions, support tickets, page views. Typically long and narrow. Every row is one event.

topics.name: Dimension tables
topics.desc: Store descriptive context — customers, products, dates, regions. Typically wide and short. Every row describes one entity.

topics.name: Normalization in Power BI
topics.desc: Split wide flat tables into proper fact and dimension tables before loading. Your model performance and DAX complexity both depend on this.

topics.name: Calculated columns
topics.desc: A new column computed row by row at model refresh time. Stored in memory. Use for categorisation and segmentation logic.

topics.name: Measures
topics.desc: Calculations evaluated at query time depending on filter context. This is where DAX lives. All your KPIs should be measures, never calculated columns.

topics.name: Basic DAX functions
topics.desc: SUM, AVERAGE, COUNT, COUNTROWS, DISTINCTCOUNT, MIN, MAX, DIVIDE. These are the building blocks of 80% of business metrics.

topics.name: CALCULATE
topics.desc: The most powerful function in DAX. Evaluates an expression in a modified filter context. Every advanced DAX pattern uses CALCULATE.

topics.name: FILTER
topics.desc: Returns a filtered table. Used inside CALCULATE to apply complex conditions that cannot be expressed as simple column filters.

topics.name: ALL & ALLEXCEPT
topics.desc: ALL removes all filters from a table or column. ALLEXCEPT removes all filters except the ones you specify. Foundation of % of total calculations.

topics.name: RELATED & USERELATIONSHIP
topics.desc: RELATED follows a relationship to retrieve a column value from a related table. USERELATIONSHIP activates an inactive relationship for a specific measure.

topics.name: Variables in DAX
topics.desc: VAR and RETURN. Define intermediate calculations once, reference them multiple times. Makes complex DAX readable and eliminates repeated evaluation.

topics.name: Iterators (SUMX, AVERAGEX)
topics.desc: Functions that iterate row by row over a table before aggregating. SUMX(Sales, Sales[Qty] * Sales[Price]) is a classic example. Essential for row-level calculations.

topics.name: Time Intelligence
topics.desc: TOTALYTD, SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR, DATEADD, PARALLELPERIOD. Requires a proper date table. The foundation of every financial and sales report.

topics.name: KPI Cards
topics.desc: Single-metric callouts at the top of every dashboard. The first thing an executive looks at. Design them to communicate direction, not just value.

topics.name: Tables & Matrix
topics.desc: Tabular views for detailed breakdown. Matrix supports row and column hierarchies with subtotals. Essential for financial reports.

topics.name: Charts & Maps
topics.desc: Bar, line, scatter, treemap, funnel, map visuals. Choose based on the relationship you are communicating, not what looks impressive.

topics.name: Drillthroughs
topics.desc: Right-click a data point to navigate to a detail page filtered to that context. Adds investigation capability without cluttering your summary page.

topics.name: Tooltips
topics.desc: Custom tooltip pages that appear on hover. Show a sparkline, a mini table, or additional context without adding elements to your main canvas.

topics.name: Bookmarks
topics.desc: Saved states of your report page — filter selections, visible visuals, page positions. Used to build navigation buttons and storytelling flows.

topics.name: Executive, Analytical & Operational dashboards
topics.desc: Three distinct design targets. Executives want headlines. Analysts want depth. Operations wants real-time status. The same data, three different designs.

topics.name: Publishing reports
topics.desc: Publish from Desktop to the Service. The report is live — changes in Desktop require republishing. Understand the Desktop vs Service distinction.

topics.name: Workspaces & Apps
topics.desc: Workspaces are development environments. Apps are packaged collections of reports published to end users. Separate the two in your workflow.

topics.name: Scheduled refresh
topics.desc: Configure your dataset to refresh automatically on a schedule. Requires a gateway for on-premises sources. The difference between a live dashboard and a static one.

topics.name: Sharing reports
topics.desc: Share links, embed in Teams, publish to web. Each method has different access control implications. Know who can see what before you share anything.

topics.name: Row Level Security (RLS)
topics.desc: Filter data by user identity. A sales manager sees only their region. Build RLS into your model, not as an afterthought after deployment.

topics.name: Access Control
topics.desc: Workspace roles — Admin, Member, Contributor, Viewer. Assign the minimum access required. Principle of least privilege applies to BI too.

topics.name: Data Lineage
topics.desc: Visualise the flow from data source through dataflows to datasets to reports. Critical for impact analysis before changing an upstream dataset.

topics.name: Endorsement
topics.desc: Certified or Promoted tags that signal to users which datasets are the trusted, official source of truth. Prevents shadow datasets from proliferating.

topics.name: Copilot in Power BI
topics.desc: Generate report pages, write DAX measures, and summarise insights from natural language prompts. Available in Premium workspaces.

topics.name: Q&A Visual
topics.desc: Users type questions in plain English and Power BI generates the answer as a visual. Train the Q&A engine with synonyms for your business terminology.

topics.name: Claude for DAX writing
topics.desc: Share your model schema and describe the measure you need. Claude writes production-ready DAX with variable naming, comments, and edge case handling.

topics.name: ChatGPT for debugging
topics.desc: Paste a broken DAX measure and the error. AI diagnoses the filter context issue, explains what went wrong, and rewrites the fix.

projects.title: Operation Clearwater — Power BI
projects.desc: Fraud detection and risk intelligence dashboard for NorthAxis Bank. Composite risk scoring, watchlist, and board-ready executive summary.
projects.icon: <svg width="18" height="18" viewBox="0 0 18 18" fill="none"><rect x="2" y="2" width="14" height="14" rx="2.5" fill="#C9A227" fill-opacity="0.1" stroke="#C9A227" stroke-width="1.4"/><rect x="5" y="11" width="2.5" height="4" rx="1" fill="#C9A227"/><rect x="8.5" y="8" width="2.5" height="7" rx="1" fill="#C9A227" opacity=".75"/><rect x="12" y="5" width="2.5" height="10" rx="1" fill="#C9A227" opacity=".5"/></svg>

projects.title: AthleteX Care Dashboard
projects.desc: Sports injury analytics dashboard tracking injury trends, treatment costs, and recovery outcomes across clubs, events, and regions.
projects.icon: <svg width="18" height="18" viewBox="0 0 18 18" fill="none"><circle cx="9" cy="9" r="7" stroke="#C9A227" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="9" cy="9" r="4" stroke="#C9A227" stroke-width="1.5" opacity=".6"/><circle cx="9" cy="9" r="1.5" fill="#C9A227"/></svg>

title: Business Communication & Stakeholder Management
subtitle: The hardest skill to teach. The most important one to have.

topics.name: Discovery sessions
topics.desc: Structured conversations to understand what a stakeholder actually needs — often different from what they initially ask for. Listen more than you speak.

topics.name: Stakeholder interviews
topics.desc: One-on-one conversations to surface requirements, pain points, and decision-making context. The quality of your questions determines the quality of your analysis.

topics.name: Workshop facilitation
topics.desc: Group sessions to align multiple stakeholders on requirements, priorities, and definitions. Conflict surfaces here — that is the point.

topics.name: Requirements elicitation
topics.desc: The formal process of drawing out unstated, implicit, and conflicting requirements. Most stakeholders do not know exactly what they need until they see what they do not want.

topics.name: Functional requirements
topics.desc: What the system or analysis must do. Specific, measurable, testable. "Show monthly revenue by region" is functional. "Make a good dashboard" is not.

topics.name: Non-functional requirements
topics.desc: How the system must perform — refresh frequency, load time, accessibility, user volume. Ignored at the start, complained about at the end.

topics.name: KPI definition
topics.desc: Documenting exactly how each metric is calculated, what data source it comes from, and who owns it. Prevents the "your number is different from my number" problem.

topics.name: Success criteria
topics.desc: How will you and the stakeholder agree that this project succeeded? Define it before you start. Reference it at delivery.

topics.name: BRD — Business Requirements Document
topics.desc: Captures the business need, scope, and high-level requirements. Written before any technical work begins. The contract between you and the stakeholder.

topics.name: FRD — Functional Requirements Document
topics.desc: Translates business requirements into specific, technical functional specifications. The bridge between business intent and technical delivery.

topics.name: User stories
topics.desc: As a [user], I want [capability] so that [benefit]. Keeps requirements grounded in who uses the output and why it matters to them.

topics.name: Data dictionaries
topics.desc: A catalogue of every field in your dataset — name, data type, description, source, and business definition. Non-negotiable for shared datasets.

topics.name: KPI documentation
topics.desc: A formal record of every metric — formula, data source, refresh cadence, owner, and known limitations. Prevents confusion when the same metric returns different values from different tools.

topics.name: Managing expectations
topics.desc: Scope creep starts when expectations are not set. Be explicit about what is included, what is excluded, and what the timeline is. In writing.

topics.name: Handling conflicting requirements
topics.desc: Different stakeholders want different things. Your job is not to pick a side — it is to facilitate alignment. Document the conflict and escalate if necessary.

topics.name: Prioritization
topics.desc: Not everything can be done first. Use a structured framework — impact vs effort, must have vs nice to have. Get stakeholders to rank, not just list.

topics.name: Scope management
topics.desc: Every addition to scope is a subtraction from something else. Make the trade-off visible every time a new request comes in.

topics.name: Email writing
topics.desc: Lead with the ask or the answer, not the context. Busy stakeholders read the first two lines and decide whether to continue. Write accordingly.

topics.name: Meeting facilitation
topics.desc: Every meeting needs an agenda, an owner, and a decision or action output. If a meeting can be an email, it should be.

topics.name: Presentation skills
topics.desc: Structure before slides. What is the one thing you need this audience to believe or do when you are done? Build backward from that.

topics.name: Executive communication
topics.desc: Executives communicate in headlines and implications. Lead with the finding, then the evidence, then the recommendation. Never lead with methodology.

title: PowerPoint for Data
subtitle: Your analysis is only as valuable as your ability to communicate it.

topics.name: Structuring data stories
topics.desc: Situation, complication, resolution. Every data presentation follows this arc. Context first, then the problem, then what the data says to do.

topics.name: The narrative arc
topics.desc: Data without a story is a spreadsheet. Identify the tension in your findings — what is happening that should not be, or what is not happening that should — and build from there.

topics.name: Building compelling insights
topics.desc: An insight answers "so what?" — not just what the number is, but what it means for the business and what should happen next.

topics.name: Layout principles
topics.desc: One message per slide. If you have two things to say, you have two slides. Density is the enemy of comprehension.

topics.name: Visual hierarchy
topics.desc: The headline should be the insight, not a label. "West region declining 18% MoM" is a headline. "Regional Performance" is a label. These are not the same.

topics.name: Typography
topics.desc: Two fonts maximum. One for headlines, one for body. Size signals importance. Bold for emphasis. Never use more than three text sizes on one slide.

topics.name: Color consistency
topics.desc: Pick a palette and use it everywhere. One accent color for highlights. Consistent use of red for negative, green for positive, grey for context.

topics.name: Board-level reporting
topics.desc: Three slides maximum for the core message — situation, finding, recommendation. Everything else is backup. Boards make decisions, not analysis.

topics.name: Leadership presentations
topics.desc: Open with the conclusion. Executives do not have time to wait for the build-up. State the finding, then prove it.

topics.name: Decision-focused storytelling
topics.desc: Every slide should point toward a decision. If a slide does not contribute to the conclusion, cut it. Ruthlessly.

topics.name: Choosing the right visual
topics.desc: Match the chart to the relationship. Comparison needs a bar chart, not a pie. Trend needs a line, not a bar. Get this wrong and the audience draws the wrong conclusion.

topics.name: Simplifying complex information
topics.desc: If your chart needs a legend longer than three items, redesign it. If it needs more than 10 seconds to interpret, it has too much information.

topics.name: Presenting to executives
topics.desc: Pause after the headline. Let it land. Do not rush to explain before they have processed it. Silence in a presentation is not dead air — it is impact.

topics.name: Presenting to non-technical stakeholders
topics.desc: Avoid jargon. Use analogies. Translate every statistical finding into a business implication. "A p-value of 0.03" means nothing — "this is very unlikely to be random" means something.

topics.name: Handling questions confidently
topics.desc: Know your data well enough to answer follow-up questions. Anticipate the three most likely challenges. "I do not know, I will find out" is always better than a wrong answer.

topics.name: Converting cluttered slides
topics.desc: Remove every element that does not serve the message — gridlines, legends that repeat what is labeled, decorative images, text boxes that explain the obvious.

topics.name: Executive-ready presentations
topics.desc: One insight per slide. Insight headline at the top. Supporting visual below. Recommendation at the bottom. Consistent format. Nothing extra.

title: Python for Analytics
subtitle: Automation, depth, and scale. Everything Excel cannot do at volume.

topics.name: VS Code
topics.desc: The industry-standard code editor. Lightweight, extensible, supports Jupyter notebooks natively. Install the Python and Pylance extensions before anything else.

topics.name: Jupyter Notebook
topics.desc: Interactive coding environment where code and output live together. The standard for data analysis and EDA. Each cell is independently executable.

topics.name: Anaconda
topics.desc: A distribution that comes pre-loaded with Python and 250+ data science libraries. Removes the setup friction for beginners. Includes conda for environment management.

topics.name: Virtual Environments
topics.desc: Isolated Python environments for each project. Prevents dependency conflicts between projects. Always create a virtual environment before installing libraries.

topics.name: Variables & Data Types
topics.desc: Integers, floats, strings, booleans, lists, dictionaries, tuples. How you store data in Python determines what you can do with it.

topics.name: Loops
topics.desc: for loops and while loops. Automate repetitive tasks. Iterate over rows, files, or API responses. The foundation of automation thinking.

topics.name: Functions
topics.desc: Reusable blocks of code. Define once, call everywhere. Functions are what separate scripts from professional code.

topics.name: Conditional Statements
topics.desc: if, elif, else. Control the flow of your code based on conditions. The same logic as CASE in SQL and IF in Excel — but far more powerful.

topics.name: Loading data
topics.desc: pd.read_csv(), pd.read_excel(), pd.read_sql(). Read data from virtually any source into a DataFrame in one line.

topics.name: Exploring data
topics.desc: df.head(), df.info(), df.describe(), df.value_counts(). Profile your dataset before touching it. Know its shape, types, and distribution.

topics.name: Cleaning data
topics.desc: Handle nulls, fix data types, remove duplicates, standardise formats. Pandas is faster and more auditable than Excel for large datasets.

topics.name: Transforming data
topics.desc: Apply functions to columns, create new columns, reshape DataFrames with pivot and melt. ETL logic without a pipeline tool.

topics.name: Aggregations
topics.desc: groupby(), agg(), pivot_table(). The Pandas equivalent of GROUP BY and Pivot Tables. The building block of every summary analysis.

topics.name: Arrays
topics.desc: NumPy's core data structure. Faster and more memory-efficient than Python lists for numerical operations. The foundation that Pandas is built on.

topics.name: Mathematical operations
topics.desc: Vectorised operations that run on entire arrays without loops. np.sum(), np.mean(), np.std() — faster than iterating row by row.

topics.name: Statistical analysis
topics.desc: np.percentile(), np.corrcoef(), np.random. The building blocks for statistical computation that Pandas delegates to NumPy under the hood.

topics.name: Matplotlib
topics.desc: The foundational Python visualization library. Verbose but fully controllable. Understanding Matplotlib makes every other library easier — they are all built on it.

topics.name: Seaborn
topics.desc: Statistical visualization built on Matplotlib. Beautiful defaults, built-in support for distributions, correlations, and categorical plots with minimal code.

topics.name: Exploratory visualizations
topics.desc: Quick charts during EDA — histograms, scatter plots, boxplots, correlation heatmaps. The goal is to understand the data, not impress anyone.

topics.name: Business visualizations
topics.desc: Polished charts for reports and presentations. Consistent colour scheme, labeled axes, informative titles, annotations that highlight the key insight.

topics.name: Profiling datasets
topics.desc: Systematic exploration — shape, dtypes, null counts, unique value counts, distributions, correlations. Never skip this step on a new dataset.

topics.name: Identifying patterns
topics.desc: Segment comparisons, time series decomposition, outlier detection, correlation analysis. EDA is where hypotheses are formed, not confirmed.

topics.name: Feature exploration
topics.desc: Understanding each column — its range, distribution, relationship to other columns, and potential as an analytical dimension.

topics.name: Reading & writing files
topics.desc: pd.read_csv(), df.to_excel(), open(), json.dump(). Automating file I/O is what makes Python useful for operational workflows.

topics.name: Scheduled reporting
topics.desc: Combine Python scripts with task schedulers (cron, Windows Task Scheduler) to run reports automatically and email the output.

topics.name: Workflow automation
topics.desc: Python replaces manual, repetitive analyst work — downloading files, transforming data, generating reports, sending updates. Automate the routine, focus on the insight.

topics.name: Claude for code generation
topics.desc: Describe what you want your script to do. Claude writes the full function, handles edge cases, and explains each part. Accelerates development dramatically.

topics.name: ChatGPT for debugging
topics.desc: Paste the error traceback and the relevant code block. AI identifies the root cause and rewrites the fix — often catching multiple issues in one pass.

topics.name: Automation with AI
topics.desc: Use AI to generate boilerplate automation code — file processing pipelines, email sending scripts, data validation routines — that you then customise.

topics.name: Prompt patterns for Python
topics.desc: The context you give AI determines the quality of the code you get. Schema, sample data, expected output, and constraint all belong in the prompt.

title: Analytics Domains
subtitle: The tools are the same. The questions are different. Pick a lane.

topics.name: Sales Analytics
topics.desc: Pipeline analysis, revenue tracking, conversion rates, sales rep performance, forecast vs actual. The most common analytics use case in commercial businesses.

topics.name: Marketing Analytics
topics.desc: Campaign performance, attribution modelling, CAC by channel, funnel analysis, A/B testing. Connects marketing spend to measurable business outcomes.

topics.name: Product Analytics
topics.desc: User behavior, feature adoption, retention curves, funnel analysis, DAU/MAU. Most common in tech companies and SaaS products.

topics.name: Finance Analytics
topics.desc: Budget vs actual, variance analysis, forecasting, P&L reporting, cash flow modelling. High-stakes, zero-tolerance for errors.

topics.name: Operations Analytics
topics.desc: SLA adherence, productivity metrics, process bottlenecks, supply chain performance, logistics tracking. The domain where milliseconds and percentages have direct cost implications.

topics.name: HR Analytics
topics.desc: Employee retention, recruitment funnel efficiency, headcount planning, performance distribution, compensation benchmarking. Growing fast as companies become data-driven about their people.

topics.name: Pick one domain to go deep on
topics.desc: A generalist analyst competes with everyone. A finance analytics specialist competes with far fewer people — and commands higher rates. Choose early.

topics.name: Build domain-specific projects
topics.desc: Two projects in sales analytics signal specialisation. One project in each domain signals someone who has not committed. Depth wins.

topics.name: Create domain-specific content
topics.desc: Write about the problems, metrics, and data structures of your chosen domain. Content establishes you as a domain expert faster than any certification.

topics.name: Become the go-to person in your niche
topics.desc: When someone in your network has a finance analytics problem, you want your name to be the first one they think of. That requires visible, consistent specialisation.

title: AI Integration
subtitle: AI does not replace the analyst. It eliminates the analyst who refuses to use it.

topics.name: How LLMs work
topics.desc: Large Language Models predict the next token based on context. They do not reason — they pattern-match at extraordinary scale. Understanding this shapes how you use them.

topics.name: AI limitations
topics.desc: Hallucination, context window limits, inability to access real-time data, sensitivity to prompt wording. Know the failure modes before depending on the output.

topics.name: Responsible AI use
topics.desc: Do not share confidential data with AI tools. Verify every output before using it in production. AI is a draft generator — you are the editor.

topics.name: Structured prompting
topics.desc: Role + context + task + format + constraints. A structured prompt returns a usable output. A vague prompt returns a generic one.

topics.name: Chain-of-thought prompting
topics.desc: Ask the model to think step by step before answering. Dramatically improves accuracy on complex analytical and logical tasks.

topics.name: Context engineering
topics.desc: The quality of your prompt context determines the quality of the output. Schema, sample data, business definitions, and constraints belong in every analytical prompt.

topics.name: Data cleaning with AI
topics.desc: Paste a sample of messy data and ask for a cleaning strategy. AI identifies patterns you might miss and suggests transformation logic.

topics.name: Data analysis with AI
topics.desc: Share aggregated results and ask for interpretation, anomaly detection, and hypotheses. AI as a first-pass analyst — you validate and deepen.

topics.name: Dashboard generation
topics.desc: Describe your dataset and business question. AI suggests the right visuals, layout, and KPIs before you open Power BI.

topics.name: Insight generation
topics.desc: Share your findings and ask AI to help you articulate the business implication and recommended action. Speeds up the "so what?" step dramatically.

topics.name: Claude
topics.desc: Best for long-context analytical work — reading documentation, writing complex SQL, building structured prompts, and working with code files. Anthropic.

topics.name: ChatGPT
topics.desc: Broad general-purpose capability. Strong for code generation, debugging, and drafting communications. GPT-4 and above for analytical work. OpenAI.

topics.name: Microsoft Copilot
topics.desc: Native integration across Excel, PowerPoint, Teams, and Power BI. The most frictionless AI for analysts already in the Microsoft ecosystem.

topics.name: NotebookLM
topics.desc: Google's tool for analysing and querying documents and research. Upload reports, transcripts, and data dictionaries and ask questions directly.

topics.name: Perplexity
topics.desc: AI-powered search with cited sources. Faster than Google for researching unfamiliar domains, benchmarks, and industry metrics.

topics.name: AI-assisted workflows
topics.desc: Map every repetitive step in your current workflow and identify which ones AI can accelerate. Start there. Compound over time.

topics.name: Automated reporting
topics.desc: Python + AI + scheduled refresh — reports that write, format, and distribute themselves. The analyst's time shifts from production to interpretation.

topics.name: Human + AI collaboration
topics.desc: AI handles first drafts, exploration, and documentation. You handle judgment, context, stakeholder communication, and accountability for conclusions.

topics.name: AI-first analytics workflows
topics.desc: Start every project by asking AI what questions you should be asking, what data you need, and what the standard analytical approach is. Then go deeper.

title: Career Development
subtitle: Skills get you in the room. Positioning gets you the offer.

topics.name: GitHub — document every project
topics.desc: Every project you build belongs on GitHub with a proper README — problem statement, approach, findings, tools used. Recruiters check this.

topics.name: Power BI Portfolio
topics.desc: Publish your dashboards to Power BI Service and share the link. Screenshots are not enough — let them interact with the work.

topics.name: Project documentation & case studies
topics.desc: Write up every project as a case study — the business problem, your analytical approach, the findings, and the recommendation. This is what separates you from candidates with only code.

topics.name: Building in public on LinkedIn & X
topics.desc: Post every project. Share what you learned. Document the process, not just the output. Visibility compounds. The analyst who posts is more hireable than the equally skilled one who does not.

topics.name: ATS optimization
topics.desc: Most resumes are screened by software before a human sees them. Use exact keywords from the job description. SQL, Power BI, Python — spell them as they appear in job postings.

topics.name: Analytics resume structure
topics.desc: Name, title, summary, skills, experience, projects, education. Lead with the impact of each role — percentages, scale, outcomes — not a list of responsibilities.

topics.name: Project presentation
topics.desc: Each project on your resume should have a one-line summary, the tools used, and one measurable outcome. If you cannot quantify the impact, you are not describing the project correctly.

topics.name: LinkedIn personal branding
topics.desc: Headline, About section, featured section, and consistent posting. Your LinkedIn is a living portfolio — treat it that way.

topics.name: Content creation for visibility
topics.desc: Post SQL concepts, dashboard teardowns, project walkthroughs. The people who post become the people who get opportunities. It is that direct.

topics.name: Networking strategy
topics.desc: Comment on posts in your domain. Connect with people whose work you admire and say something specific. DM with context, not just "I want to connect."

topics.name: SQL interviews
topics.desc: Window functions, GROUP BY vs HAVING, JOIN types, query optimisation. Practice on LeetCode and StrataScratch. Write the query, then explain your thought process.

topics.name: Statistics interviews
topics.desc: Explain the difference between correlation and causation, when to use median vs mean, what a p-value means in business terms. Conceptual clarity matters more than formulas.

topics.name: Power BI interviews
topics.desc: CALCULATE, filter context, relationship directions, RLS implementation. Be able to walk through a data model design and explain every decision.

topics.name: Business case interviews
topics.desc: Given a metric that dropped 20%, how do you investigate? Frame your approach before touching data. Hypothesis first, analysis second. This is what senior roles test.

topics.name: Fiverr & Upwork
topics.desc: Start with small, well-scoped projects. One strong review compounds. Specialize your profile — "Power BI dashboards for e-commerce" beats "data analyst."

topics.name: Consulting fundamentals
topics.desc: Scope clearly. Price for value, not time. Deliver on or before the deadline. Communication is more important than the work — most clients will not know if your DAX is elegant, but they will notice if you go quiet.

topics.name: Junior Analyst → Analyst
topics.desc: Own your deliverables end to end. Ask fewer clarifying questions. Proactively surface findings before being asked. Initiative is the signal for promotion readiness.

topics.name: Analyst → Senior Analyst
topics.desc: Your output starts influencing strategy, not just reporting on it. You define the metrics, not just calculate them. You manage stakeholder relationships independently.

topics.name: Senior Analyst → Analytics Lead
topics.desc: You are building the system, not working in it. Defining the data infrastructure, the reporting standards, the team's analytical approach. Technical depth plus business leadership.
